references.title: Unpacking help-seeking process through multimodal learning analytics: A comparative study of ChatGPT vs Human expert

extracted.Data sources: LMS logs, Eye-Tracking Data, Conversational transcripts
extracted.Features: [Diagnosing Question, Asking Help Instrumental, Asking Help Executive, Asking Help Avoidant, Asking Help Clarifying, Asking Help Understanding, Evaluating Help Positive, Evaluating Help No Feedback, Evaluating Help Negative, Processing Help Accepting, Processing Help Neglecting, Processing Help Returning]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Data collected from the different sources is aggregated into each construct of help-seeking. 

The processes are measured in relative terms.
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model:
Differences between HE group and ChatGPT is done using a Shapiro-Wilk test. Effect size of AI presence is done using Cliff’s delta.

FOMM rules needed at least a 5% threshold of transitions.
extracted.Implications:
Students wityh access to AI have shown a much higher propensity to ask for help than their peers. LEarners without access to it are much more likely to critically try to engage with questions before going through with asking for help.

Moreover, students without access to AI are much more likely to follow through with the standard linear  help-seeking process. Access to AI often skips critical steps: Diagnosing Question and Evaluating Answer.
extracted.Comments:
Work does a comparison between help-seeking behaviors for essay writing of learners using ChatGPT vs students who do not have access to ChatGPT.

Groups were set experimentally Apriori.
extracted.Limitations:
Difficulty in measuring cognitive processes exclusively using behavioral data.

Human interactions were not done in a naturalistic classroom setting. 

Small sample size in the context of a single course.

No analysis of context of quality of the help provided (authors measure the act of seeking help). 

Requires validation for learning tasks beyond essay writing.

annotations.text: There is close to no research highlighting differences in help-seeking behaviour when it comes to asking help to humans or to GenAI. Authors use multi-modal data to compare help-seeking between 2 groups of students. Comparison is done using Statistical tests and process mining.

Results show that Human help-seeking is linear, whilst AI group follows a more non-linear approach to help-seeking. Also, in human help-seeking there seems to be more thought put into the analysis of the feedback that is received, showing that it is possible that student over-rely on AI when it is available (since they ask pretty much everything).
